comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's a little shy of midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. This week's classic book of Western occultism is The Kybalion, the most enduring of the many works that blended occultism with New Thought. (New Thought? That's the classic American movement of self-help through mind training that emerged after the Civil War as people started paying attention to the way that thinking shapes experience and emotions affect health.)  It's the original source of the Seven Hermetic Principles -- the Principles of Mentalism, Correspondence, Vibration, Polarity, Rhythm, Cause and Effect, and Gender -- and of many other core concepts of 20th century American occultism. 

According to the cover, The Kybalion was written by "Three Initiates;" in fact, it was written by one, the redoubtable William Walker Atkinson, one of the most influential American occultists of his time. If Hermes can be Thrice Great, no doubt Atkinson can be Thrice Initiate...

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you. See you next week!***

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What are the differences in philosphy between Renaissance Hermeticism and the English Hermeticism that gave rise to the Golden Dawn?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The difficulty in answering that question is that Renaissance Hermeticism was a very broad movement that incorporated many different philosophical outlooks, from the Neoplatonism of Ficino and Agrippa through the ecstatic pietism of Jakob Boehme and beyond. The one pervasive difference, though, is that in the Renaissance the intellectual impact of Copernicanism still hadn't really hit, and most people -- even if they'd encountered the notion of a heliocentric cosmology -- still lived mentally in the orderly, enclosed geocentric universe of Ptolemy. By the time the currents that became the Golden Dawn began to take shape, all that was a matter of distant memory, and people -- including occultists -- lived mentally on a planet whirling through immensities of space. The result was a much greater focus on magic as an act of the individual will, rather than as participation in a cosmic process -- the keynote of Renaissance magic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Another difference is that, Renaissance Hermeticists had a more positive attitude towards science and had direct involvement in progressive movements of their time. Major proponents of the heliocentric theory (like Copernicus, Bruno, Campanella, Galileo etc.) were associated with Hermeticism, one way or another. It is also often speculated that the heliocentric theory emerged as a part of the social reformation programme of Renaissance Hermeticists and Proto-Rosicrucians (here is an example: http://pansophers.com/threefoldnature-proto-rc/ ) This could well be one of the reasons why Catholic Church saw heliocentrism as a serious threat to its authority, rather than a mere alternative cosmological hypothesis.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Is it possible to hold both conceptions as valid, i.e. "true", say for example if you are working with Renaissance magic?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Of course. When I do Renaissance astrological magic I put myself as far as possible into the mindset of the Renaissance occultist, and I get reliably good results.

Good evening JMG,

1. I had a most enjoyable experience just now doing the SOP in a dark room, as in it being night time with the lights turned off. Would practicing it in this manner pose any particular problems in your experience? For me it made visualization of each of the 6 glyphs a bit easier and more vivid for me.

2. Not really a question per-se, but rather a personal data point I'd like to share with you and everyone here. SOP has been helping me greatly with my lifelong moodiness and crankiness issues. Before doing spiritual practices I would too often knee-jerk react to the crummy energies around me and sometimes snap at people in ugly ways. But now it seems like when I come into contact with other people's negative energies I'm able to internally process these emotional sensations in a more detached manner and actually observe them as internal phenomena rather than just blindly let these feelings make me react in a predictable and unreflective manner. I've thought about this and it seems the main problem has been a rather unclean astral (or etheric??) environment I've been in close quarters with some quite some time. I've always been extremely sensitive to other people's moods and habits and at times have become a recluse to avoid the emotional weather (or maybe just weird vibes) other people emit. It really does seem the unseen environment out there really is a colossal mess! And hopefully I'm now doing my tiny little part of make the area immediately around me less unpleasant. SOP was very subtle at first, but the cumulative effects really seem to be stacking up now that I've been at it for about 6 months now.

3. This one is a question. I've been studying up on the 7 elements and the 8-spehere/wheel chart as you present them in DMH. Some questions:

(a) At first glance some of the correspondences strike me as a bit novel (or at least unconventional) when compared to more established systems. For example, associating Saturn with the spring equinox, Venus with Air (tbh, I've seen different systems present totally different hot takes on what Venus corresponds to), and Mercury with Spirit (ether??. Out of curiosity, how did you arrive at the correspondences in this system come about? I do realize that your system does seem to bring out different aspects of the elements and planets and in general the planets are probably way too complex and multifaceted to be associated with just one element. Did I just answer my own question?

(b)I've seen another magical system that uses 7 elements and instead of Spirit Below and above, the added elements (to the usual 4) are Wood, Metal, and Spirit. are used. As these getting at the same sort of thing as the SOP elements?

(c) Is there a direct correlation between the 7 SOP elements and the 8 spheres of the calendar wheel? Obviously one set has 7 and the other, 8. Which element of the wheel is left out of the ritual? Is Mercury/Spirit? Or is that fused with the 3 spirit elements?

So far I've figured:
Gwyar = Spirit Within / Lunar Current
Nwyfre = Spirit Above / Solar Current
Calas = Spirit Below / Telluric Current
Earth = Earth/North
Water = Water/West
Fire = Fire/South
Air = Air/East
Spirit = All 3 spirit elements?

This sound about right? Apologies if this is pedantic, as my brain tends to go into OCD mode when presented with elements and correspondences.

comments.title: Mental disjunct sets
comments.content:
When I was little I often wondered why so many adults were so blind to many things that seemed obvious. As an adult now I see that myself, have been victim to the effects of this blindness. I now know, thanks to you by the way, how to remove much of the crud and maintain my sanity, which I almost lost after moving to the US from a small, tranquil (ish) Mexican town, to the point that once I was almost hallucinating as when someone has very high fever. I’ve been ruminating with this thought and have been on and on trying to figure out why this happens to me more so than others I know. Using occult language, if I may do so without knowing exactly if it makes sense, I think I am very sensitive to the lower astral apparently, or to the planes that involve thoughts and ideas. For example if somebody throws a thought at me I’ll follow it through, and sometimes it will just get stuck and my mind will go to the point of blowing steam and I have a hard time, much less than before, putting it down or when I am thinking or solving a problem I will just almost frenetically continue until I have reached what I would consider the end of the thought. This, as I mentioned and now that I look back increased exponentially when moving out here. A few questions arise from this and related experiences.

1) I am not sure I understand correctly the difference between astral and mental. I understand that astral es the realm of dreams and some thought but then the mental, if I recall correctly, is more concerned with the basic building patterns of thought. What does the matter mean?
2) Using my mind is one of the things I value about myself but I feel my mind is strong in an unbalanced way, for example someone that has gone to the gym to build big muscles but when it comes to dexterity it just fails. What is a way of becoming a mental martial artist in order to balance and shield my mind? Discursive meditation also does this? If so I have much work to do...
3) Concerning the blindness of some thought patterns, like the one our progress-drunk society has or for example someone very rich that behaves like a yerk (convinced he is entitled) or apparently Crowley. What is this apparent disjunction that makes some thought patterns blind you against others? It also works the other way around, for example good and honest people have trouble identifying lies or lying themselves, not because they are naïve, they seem to literally not see it. Is this somehow how consciousness affects our perception and how are actions and thoughts affects our consciousness?

I think I have to stop, concerning my trouble of not being able to stop thinking to the point of a headache but, this kinda makes me think we some things seem completely outside our reach and look like gibberish until you have the right “consciousness” to actually “see”. On the other side it also is a cautionary tale from now on to me, of the dangers of not having any sort of spiritual practice in today’s world... If you are under the effect of some nasty ness, you are completely blind to it!

Many thanks, shall I say again, for your work has had a direct impact on my life and wellbeing.

replies.title: Re: Mental disjunct sets
replies.content:
1) The astral plane is the plane of concrete consciousness; the mental plane is the plane of abstract consciousness. Think of a simple mathematical equation -- say, 2 + 2 = 4. When you say that to yourself, or imagine the letters written, that's an astral experience. The meaning of it -- the unitary relationship that's expressed by those five symbols -- is an astral experience. For another example, if you learn a word in an unfamiliar language and don't know what it means, the word is a purely astral experience. Once you learn the meaning, it's a combined astral and mental experience -- and if you compare that word to other words you know that mean almost the same thing, and experience the difference in their meanings, that's a mental experience.

2) That's not a simple process! Human beings don't yet have mental bodies -- we have a much simpler structure, a mental sheath, which is in the process of evolving into a mental body. Everything you do that brings you into contact with abstract meaning, from reading a poem to thinking about a problem to practicing discursive meditation, helps with that process. There have been many systems of mind training over the years; all of them involve some degree of meditation, so that's always a good place to start.

3) Again, this is a function of our limited capacities on the mental plane. If you can learn to see yourself clearly, exactly as you are, you're most of the way to enlightenment.

replies.title: Re: Mental disjunct sets
replies.content: Let's try that fourth sentence again: "The meaning of it -- the unitary relationship that's expressed by those five symbols -- is a mental experience."

A while back someone asked about Eris as an astrological planet. I'm nowhere near the point where I can read the charts, but I've been spending a fair amount of time learning astrology lately, and I know a good deal about the history of ideas, so I started thinking: what would I want to look for before concluding there was something which would make planet Eris plausible enough to start looking into?

The idea I had was to look at the history of when each planet was discovered, and how they reshaped society. This lead me to conclude that each planet brings forth most, but by no means all, of their effects on society through a single thing.

Thus, Uranus brings forth its effects through Utopianism, by attempting to bring forth the perfect, and rejecting the establish imperfect society, radical change of a sort not seen prior the discovery of Uranus occurs. The irony of it all is that this idea which fuels Uranian cultural forms is often rejected by people with a strong Uranus, but the universe seems to have a good sense of humor, so it seems plausible.

Neptune brings forth its effects via the idea of evolution; it reveals changes, unities, but is often misunderstood; additionally, plenty of fields were revolutionized during the period when Neptune came into focus by noticing how evolution occurred, and revealing hidden linkages between things. Fields such as religious studies, biology, linguistics, and anthropology were all created or revolutionized during the time around the discovery of Uranus by evolutionary thinking; and the unities, delusions, and strange ideas which emerge from the ideas of evolution continue to bring forth many of the Neptunian elements of society to this day.

Ceres brought forth the Romantic Era, which is perhaps easiest to see due to the fact it ended, and is not part of the world anymore. At its heart, Romanticism was an embrace of the natural world, and not just as something outside of us, but as something which defines us as human beings. It was about embracing our connection to the world of nature, in all of its glory, and power.

Finally, Pluto brought forth many of its effects by the oil age. The growth in organized crime occurred due in large part to the totalitarianism of the 20th century governments; this was only possible due to the huge amounts of energy contained in oil. Nuclear power was only possible due to the oil subsidy; massive pollution, global dimming, came from oil. As did the alienation, due to car culture. The brutal wars were fought using machines powered by oil; the rejection of the natural world was also only possible due to the oil age.

There's something which got started at the period when I'd expect to see Eridian themes coming into focus, which has radically reshaped society, and which seems unlikely to last much longer: the internet. Now, what's the main use of the internet been? Porn and theft of various kinds. Both of these are strongly Eridian in nature; the world's governments either tolerate a level of lawless chaos which is horribly destructive to society in exchange for surveillance capabilities which make the KGB look lackluster, or use it for horrific, totalitarian control (or both); it's payed for in no small part by theft and sex trafficking; and the level of abuse it enables is incredible.

1) Does my analysis of how astrological planets affect society sound plausible to you?

2) Do you think it's possible the internet is evidence Eris functioned as an astrological planet?

3) I expect I'll need to spend years working on getting the basics before I can look into it further, but what would you look for to see if Eris was a planet?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I think that's a good start, but to my mind the major planets are more complex than you've suggested. Uranus, for example, didn't just bring on Utopianism. It's the planet of the individual; its discovery correlates precisely to the rise of democratic regimes and the concept of individual human rights; it's also the planet of electricity and of steam power -- check out the chronologies of both these. It's also the planet of alternative sexualities -- it was after Uranus was discovered that homosexuality changed from an activity to an identity.

2) Possibly, but it would also make sense to see the internet as the last great manifestation of the Pluto current -- it's not accidental, I think, that pornography was the first successful internet business, or that the internet lends itself so readily to so many Plutonian themes and activities.

3) You'd want to start paying attention to what happens when other planets aspect it. If it's a planet, each aspect will be marked by noticeable events that combine the flavors of the two planets.

comments.title: Another major work of W. W. Atkinson
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

The Arcane Teaching series by the same author is much more comprehensive than the Kybalion. The first book of the series also contains similar "seven laws" but it covers a wider range of topics in Western occult philosophy (and in more depth). The third book ("The Mystery of Sex or Sex Polarity") further elaborates on the principle of gender on different planes. Additionally, the second and fourth books (namely "Arcane Formulas or Mental Alchemy" and "Vril or Vital Magnetism") contain a lot practical exercises related to these teachings. replies.title: Re: Another major work of W. W. Atkinson
replies.content:
The Kybalion was kept in print steadily,. whereas The Arcane Teaching series went out of print very quickly, and was virtually unobtainable until the days of digital scans and print-on-demand services.

Also, "Vril" is the sixth in the series oif supplementary books, not the fourth, according to its title page. I have never found so much as a bare reference to the titles of the thrid, fourth and fifth books in the series; they may never have been published.

replies.title: Re: Another major work of W. W. Atkinson
replies.content:
The second edition is composed of four books (one theoretical large book plus three supplementary books). In the third edition, book-1 was divided into three smaller books (that's how "Vril" became the sixth one). The ordering that I used above is based on second edition (so "Vril" is the fourth book on that edition) that I had read.

To make sure, I quote the Wikipedia entry on that issue:
"The second edition was expanded to include three 'supplementary teachings' in pamphlet form. The four titles in this edition were: The Arcane Teaching (hardback), The Arcane Formulas, or Mental Alchemy (pamphlet), The Mystery of Sex, or Sex Polarity (pamphlet), and Vril, or Vital Magnetism (pamphlet).
(.....)
The third edition split the main title, The Arcane Teaching, into three smaller volumes, bringing the total number of books in the series to six. This edition consisted of the following titles (the three titles marked with an asterisk (*) are the volumes that had appeared together as The Arcane Teaching in the previous edition): The One and the Many* (hardback), Cosmic Law* (hardback), The Psychic Planes* (hardback), The Arcane Formulas, or Mental Alchemy (binding unknown), The Mystery of Sex, or Sex Polarity (binding unknown), and Vril, or Vital Magnetism (binding unknown)."

By the way, you can download all those four books in their second edition ordering from the following links:
http://www.yogebooks.com/english/atkinson/1909arcaneteaching.pdf
http://www.yogebooks.com/english/atkinson/1909arcaneformulas.pdf
http://iapsop.com/ssoc/1909__atkinson___the_mystery_of_sex_or_sex_polarity.pdf
http://www.yogebooks.com/english/atkinson/1911vril.pdf

replies.title: Re: Another major work of W. W. Atkinson

My own work over the years trying to track down copies using on-line library catalogs has not turned up any support whatever for most of what wikipedia claims about these several editions.  Until better evidence turns up, wikipedia seems here (as often elsewhere) to be peddling "bull-shale" as if it were gold.

replies.title: Re: Another major work of W. W. Atkinson

replies.title: Re: Magical Battle of Britain
replies.content:
My take is that Fortune's project did in fact play a very significant role in the course of the Second World War -- a narrowly defined role but a necessary one. Beginning in 1938, with the Nazi takeover of Austria, each of the countries Hitler had wanted to take over had suffered a catastrophic collapse of morale right at the critical moment. Armies that should have been able to put up an effective resistance crumpled and fled, political leaders panicked or simply surrendered, populations stared blankly as German tanks rolled past. Read Marc Bloch's Strange Defeat -- Bloch was a French officer in 1940, and chronicled the bizarre way that France's huge and competent army came totally unglued when faced with a numerically inferior German invasion -- and it's very clear that something realkly weird was going on.

Fortune's belief, and I think she was right, was that this was being engineered by magical means -- that the Nazi hierarchy had a well-staffed team of occultists working for them who set out to cause that specific effect. (The team was almost certainly part of the SS, working under Heinrich Himmler's direct supervision.) What Fortune set out to do, in turn, was to keep that from happening to Britain...and she succeeded. British morale didn't collapse; it held firm, and that's why Hitler couldn't afford to concentrate all his forces on the invasion of Russia -- he had to keep France full of soldiers and Luftwaffe squadrons to prevent Britain from invading France once his back was turned. Had Britain fallen, Hitler would have had a sufficiently large force on the eastern front in the first few months of the invasion that they very likely would have reached the Baku oilflelds...and we'd all probably be speaking German today.

Of course Fortune and her network didn't win the war by themselves. It took more than ten million Russian soldiers on the eastern front, four and a half million Allied soldiers on the western and southern fronts, the largest land battles and the largest amphibious invasion in all of history, and a few other little details like that, to crush the Nazi regime. If Britain had fallen, though, the Allied victory would have been much harder to achieve -- and the sudden solidifying of British morale in the late spring and summer of 1940 was a crucial factor in Britain's survival. To the extent that Fortune and her network contributed to that, and I believe their contribution was a significant one, they played a role in winning the war.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Almost certainly that was part of the reason they chose the imagery. T.D. Reed's book The Battle for Britain in the Fifth Century, one of the major works in the process of the rediscovery of the historical Arthur, wasn't published until 1944, but the idea that Arthur had been a real Dark Age warlord defending Britain against the Saxons was in circulation before then.

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Not that you need my thanks, but thank you once again for hosting these Q&A sessions and dealing with what are undoubtedly a lot of repetitive and boring questions.

I have some basic technical questions about the exercises in LRM:

Opening gesture

1. When performing the opening gesture, you recommend formulating the intention that your inner faculties are opening. Lately, I’ve been saying an affirmation of sorts alongside the "inner faculties are opening", which goes something like “Give me the lightning strike of true gnosis” (a sentence I got from Terence McKenna). Is this a mistake?

LBRP

2. Just to clarify, I’m not supposed to draw an actual circle on the floor, right? Only to imagine it?

3. When performing the LBRP (LRM, p.51, step 2), you mention that you should step forward from the center of the ritual space, to the circumference of the circle, to cast the banishing pentagram. However, I don’t understand where to move afterwards. The next step (3) is to “cast a blue-white line a quarter circle around the space until you reach the south.” There is no mention of moving back to the center of the circle. Is the tracing of the quarter-circle done from the point on the circumference where the pentagram was traced, or do you return to the center, trace the quarter circle, pivot south, and then move from the center to the southern point, trace the second pentagram, etc. I find that the latter possibility makes more sense.

4. Given the confusion above, I’ve been doing all the steps of the LBRP, tracing pentagrams, quarter circles and all, while standing in the center of the circle, pivoting between cardinal directions, without ever moving to the circumference of the circle. Is it OK to continue practicing like this, since I’ve already adjusted to it?

5. After performing the LBRP daily for a month, I find that my visualizations have become more individualized. For example, during step 7 of the ritual in LRM, I’m visualizing the pentagrams as being on fire and pulsating with white energy. In my mind, this fire is what gives the ritual its purifying element. Each archangel also has a distinct appearance: Raphael resembles a Swiss Guard with a silver breastplate and gold and purple tassels; Gabriel wears a blue and orange Greco-Roman style toga; Michael is dressed in Byzantine fashion, with a green cloak on top of a red robe and gladiator knee-high sandals; Auriel wears a black and olive brown robe with a hood, which conceals his face (all this is in addition to the sword, cup, staff and pentacle symbols associated with the archangels). I find that this imagery developed on its own as I practiced the ritual: I found something compelling, or something occurred to me, and I integrated it into my visualizations. Am I on the right track, or should I use more standardized representations?

6. After a month of practice, my experience of the LBRP is that it seems to “turn up the gamma” in a space (to use a metaphor from image processing), as if the space became brighter, but on a subtle level. I also feel a kind of polarization of my consciousness, as if consciousness was made up of iron strips that were suddenly exposed to a magnet and all aligned in the same direction. This is the best way that I can describe it at the moment; I hope to get a better understanding of the effects with more practice. Is this a typical experience?

Ready and study

7. When I’m selecting passages to work with, they’re often longer than a single sentence; more like short sections of 1-3 sentences each, sometimes longer, and sometimes having multiple propositions separated by semicolons (technically a single sentence!) It feels like I’m doing an injustice to the text if I try to compress them further. Should I keep going like this if it’s working for me or reduce my selections to single sentences?

8. I’ve been memorizing the sentences from my book of choice verbatim and reciting them whenever I have a spare moment. I find that this constant mental “chewing” and working out of the sentences greatly deepens my understanding of the text. It also gives the memory a solid workout. Is this too much? Should I stop memorizing them and simply keep them in mind instead?

9. I would like to work with multiple sentences a day in order to apply this approach to more than one book at the same time, but this would mean going beyond LRM. My first thought is to follow the book to the letter, as you advise all the time, but throughout LRM you mention that doing extra meditations is acceptable, so I’m thinking that extra readings would be beneficial as well, as long as I’m not mixing systems. Is this a good idea? Would I be over-straining myself?

Daily recollection

10. How bad is it to fall asleep in the middle of the daily recollection exercise? This doesn't happen too often, but every time it does, I feel that I've failed to do my work for the day. If I performed the opening gesture prior to beginning, do I need to perform the closing gesture in the morning after waking up?

11. Is it acceptable to do the daily recollection in a cross-legged meditation position while sitting in bed (with the light out)? I find that this prevents me from falling asleep and helps me to actually complete the exercise more often.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) No, just make the gesture. Don't add additional steps -- it's like deciding to add an extra cup of salt to a cake recipe!

2) There is no circle on the floor. The circle is around the outside of the space at shoulder level, drawn in the air.

3) I think you've misunderstood the instructions. What you're doing is drawing a circle around the outside of the space at shoulder level, with pentagrams at the four compass directions. So you go to the eastern edge of the space, trace the pentagram, vibrate the Name, and -- without returning to the center -- draw a quarter of that circle (around the outside of the space at shoulder level) from east to south. Repeat three more times, going to west, north, and back around to east, and you've drawn the whole pattern. Then and only then do you go back to the center.

4) It will be more useful if you take the time to figure it out and do it right.

5) This is fine, on the other hand. I suspect no two practitioners visualize the archangels the same way.

6) Don't worry about whether it's typical. Keep practicing and see where it leads you.

7) If that works for you, by all means, but make sure you're not missing subtleties in individual sentences.

8) If you get better results from memorizing, by all means.

9) You might be overstraining yourself. I'd encourage you to finish LRM first and then go on to experiment.

10) It's not a problem at all. Your subconscious mind will continue the processing as you sleep. Yes, do the closing gesture in the morning.

11) I don't recommend this. Let yourself make the transition into sleep as you do the exercise; as noted above, that's not a problem.

comments.title: Past Life Memories Surfaced, Now What?
comments.content:
Hello JMG & commentariat,

This past month I had what seems to be some past life memories surface that was triggered by a series of synchronicities & then a dream followed by more synchronicities.

A brief recap: In my study of music history the lead singer of an influential band mentioned the impact of the WWI poet Wilfred Owens on his work. Tracing that back, I looked into music of Benjamin Britten and his War Requiem that set some of those poems to music. This line of inquiry was then shelved for the time being. Then I was reading an unrelated mystery novel that happened to use the name of Wilfred Owen in a half-baked simile comparing something in the book to his poetry. Normally these kind of literary connections would leave me unphased but for some reason it made me feel a certain way and I paused, taken aback. It was like a trigger.

Then I dreamed I was in an English hospital -I'm American this time around- and I was on the receiving end of being cleaned down by a nurse. She was cleaning me, but it was also part of some kind of psyche ward treatment / punishment. I had the sense that I had done something they deemed wrong. I knew in the dream and upon awaking that this person had been a soldier in WWI. It struck me later that day that this was the surfacing of a past life memory. But I had no idea what to do with that knowledge, if anything.I'm thinking he had shell shock.

It seemed to make since of certain experiences and predilections I had in the sixth grade. For instance I read The Wasteland by Eliot around that age, and at that time had a kind of fascination with the experience of those who were in the trenches. But I've never been a military history buff. Quite far from it, I've always identified as a pacifist.

Two more weeks passed. My father, just retired, and I had been in the habit of working out together at a gym on Mondays when I got finished at my job. Except then the gym changed its hours and that didn't work. So instead we started taking walks in the cemetery. We had a few relatives in these cemeteries, so on our walks we went and found where they were. On our third visit we decided to look for my fathers great uncle who was in WWI...and the feeling that I'd been there in the past life came back as we found his gravestone in a WWI veteran section of the cemetery.

So the question is: do you think this is a past life memory resurfacing (from the dream and these synchronicities) and if so what do you do with the knowledge of a past life?

A more general part b question would be what is the point in occult lore & training of remembering our past lives to begin with? Part of the evolution of the soul I suppose.

Thanks as always.

replies.title: Re: Past Life Memories Surfaced, Now What?
replies.content:
1) That sounds very much like the beginning of the process of remembering a previous life. What do you do now? Nothing at all. Let it continue to surface as it wishes, if it wishes. You've reached a point in your spiritual development where it's helpful for you to begin to get a clearer sense of the trajectory of your soul through time, but the work you need to do is still here and now, in this life.

2) The systems of occult training I'm familiar with don't make a point of seeking out past life memories; remembering them is purely a side effect.

replies.title: Re: how to combat addiction?
replies.content:
Hey, I am turning 3 years clean this march. I'll try to share the key points that have allowed me to do so. Before anything, I would suggest to hold back on the magical practice just yet. The same way a relationship can be used for self sabotage, magical practice can become a way to distract oneself from the actual work that needs to be done.

Another key point is to allow yourself to be guided: breaking a habit involves the recognition of one's participation in its perpetuation. Rehab allows oneself to learn the ways in which one does this perpetuation, but that requires a relinquishing of control, because they way one knows to be in control is through the habit.

Hope is also key. It will get easier, but one has to live the hard parts right now. One can no longer ignore those difficulties, and it is time to deal with them. She will have help, and she will not be alone. She already has what it takes, because she already wants the help. The only thing left to do is to just live those difficulties.

Magical practice, in my opinion, will work best if started after she has about six months out of rehab. It's best if she learns the basic works of staying sober before recurring to powers outside of herself.

Finally, let her know that the hardest part, that of recognizing she needs help, is behind her, and also that she will get as much help as she allows the process to give her.

Best of luck!

JP

2) I’m working out the implications of the model of reality which asserts that there are "multiple levels of being" (material, etheric, astral, mental, spiritual, etc.) The way that most Westerners understand manifestation today, and my understanding for a long time, is derived from scientific materialism, where events in the material world are thought of as being “pushed from behind” by mechanistic causality, essentially a fancier version of the “atoms in the void” of Democritus. Does the multiple levels model imply that causality is always from the spiritual to the material? Can there still be something like purely material causality? When I hit a billiard ball with a pool cue, and this ball strikes others in a chain reaction, is this an example of material causation, or does the manifestation derive from my thought and intention to strike the ball, from higher levels of being down into the material?

3) In the last couple of MMs, you’ve fielded a lot of questions about masturbation and porn addiction. One thing that stayed with me is your observation that sexuality is something that also happens at the etheric level, not just the material level. If I understand correctly, when two people are involved, there is an etheric exchange, which leads to a feeling of energization, whereas with masturbation, the etheric body is “dumped”, leading to a feeling of exhaustion. Do you think that frequent masturbators could have chronically depleted etheric bodies, and conversely, abstinent people could have “super-charged” etheric bodies, and if so, what would be the effects of these conditions?

4) Let’s say that you are interested in studying a certain book. For illustration purposes, let's make it a technical book on occultism in some area that you're not familiar with. How would you go about it? A chapter a day while taking notes? Bingeing on it in one or two sittings? Learning seven sentences a week ala LRM? Which approach do you think leads to the optimal learning and recall? If you’re uncomfortable talking about which approach is more effective in general, then you can answer only for yourself.

5) What is the difference between the mental plane and the spiritual plane? My understanding is that the spiritual plane is similar to the Platonic world of ideas, or the world of Atziluth for the Qabalists, where the archetypes are stored prior to descending through the planes and being embodied as specific entities, but where does this leave the mental plane?

6) How would you describe the worldview of occult philosophy in terms of classical metaphysics? I don’t think it’s monistic (of either materialist or idealist kinds), nor is it strictly dualistic. Would it be pluralistic, then, with multiple independent substances like matter, ether, astral substance, mind and spirit?

7) It's interesting how the philosophy of Schopenhauer differs from that of occultism. For Schopenhauer, will is primary, and intellect is secondary. The underlying force of the universe is a blind, pointless striving, not a Logos or Mind. In fact, Schopenhauer asserts (and claims to prove) that intellect is a tool of the will, not the other way around. This is what made him such a legendary pessimist: if the world is the expression of a blind will, then human life is a "tale told by an idiot, full of sound and fury signifying nothing," and the sage will withdraw from worldly life into peaceful seclusion.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That's a very common experience. Try not to worry about it -- it takes time to get some momentum built up.

2) The magical theory is that there is no strictly material causation -- all acts have a metaphysical component.

3) That's an extremely complex field of study, but yes, chronic masturbation can result in energetic depletion leading to tiredness and depression, and strict abstinence can cause a buildup of etheric energy -- but that's not necessarily a good thing, you know. Unless the etheric energy is put to use in some way, what you've got is not too far from energetic constipation.

4) I'd read through it a couple of times and then start meditating my way through it, taking one concept at a time.

5) Nah, the mental plane is the plane of ideas. We cannot understand the spiritual plane yet, since we haven't yet evolved even the first rough sketch of a spiritual body yet.

6) It's monist -- there is always One Thing behind the world of manifestation -- but it's that kind of monism, endlessly annoying to the rationalistically minded, that insists that the One Thing is utterly beyond human comprehension and therefore we can say precisely nothing about it.

7) In occultism, the underlying force in the universe is spirit, not mind, and spirit can be seen as Will -- "God is pressure," as Dion Fortune wrote. No, the main difference between Schopenhauer and occult philosophy is that the occultist recognizes that it's just as arbitrary and meaningless to insist that the Will is blind and pointless as it would be to insist that it's purposeful and teleological. The Will, spirit, God as pressure, is, and our concepts of meaning and meaninglessness are relevant solely to the mental habits of some not very bright social primates on a small rock whirling around an ordinary star off somewhere in a stray corner of the cosmos. Nietzsche's critique of dogmatic philosophy is spot on here as elsewhere -- too many philosophers project their habits of thinking into the cosmos and think they're seeing eternal truths. As Nietzsche suggested, every philosophy is simply a disguised biographical sketch of its inventor...

Once you mentioned that Russian materialism had a different stance about psychic phenomena than Western materialism. I think a similar type of difference can be seen by looking at the angle between classical Marxism and Leninism. Marx thought that proletariat would attain class consciousness only through changes in its material conditions (such as economic crises and worsening of living standards). However, Lenin thought that changes in the material conditions wouldn't be sufficient for it; he proposed that the vanguard party must develop a strong willpower and use it to catalyze changes in the class consciousness of proletariat.
I don't know if Lenin had any acquaintance with occult philosophy, but he somehow stumbled upon an essential aspect of practical occultism: the use of will/intent to cause changes in the consciousness.

Reversed Ruis
Reversed Coll

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
"This week's classic book of Western occultism is The Kybalion, the most enduring of the many works that blended occultism with New Thought."

Did the early New Thought itself (before Atkinson) have occult foundations? (I heard that it was influenced by Transcendentalism and Swedenborgianism, but I'm not sure if these imply direct occult foundations.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
New Thought was a product of 19th-century New England, which had been a bubbling cauldron of religious eccentricity and popular occultism ever since the 1600s, everywhere except in the most extremely Puritan churchgoing circles of Massachusetts and Connecticut. Catherine Albanese has traced some of this in her massive "A Republic of Mind and Spirit" (2008), but to my mind she over-emphasizes the influence of the New-England intellectual elite in general, and of Transcendentalism in particular. Yankee Swedenborgianism seems (to me, at least) to have been more influential than Transcendentalism: Swedenborgians were everywhere in 19th-century rural New England, as were Deists and Free-Thinkers of various persuasions.

As for New-Thought churches and other such religious institutions, they arose in the last quarter of the 19th century: Mary Baker Eddy's Christian Science was the first one of any lasting significance, but there were many others: Divine Science, Unity, High Watch Fellowship, etc. etc. Malinda Eliot Cramer (founder of Divine Science), for example, had a strong interest in various forms of occultism, Rosicrucianism (chiefly Freeman Dowd's variety, IIRC) and Gnosticism. Atkinson was merely one of the deepest thinkers and best writers who came out of this rich milieu.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What Robert said. America in the 19th century was so full of occultists you could toss a rock out of a train in most parts of the country and have a good chance of hitting one, and it would have taken remarkable restraint for New Thought not to have drawn on occult influences.

replies.title: Re: A good read / a cat situation
replies.content:
I too will keep Tibby in my prayers. Here is a resource for veterinary homeopathy https://theavh.org/. Some old books are available on the Internet Archive but when dealing with serious conditions experienced advice is best. That said, some have observed that Phosphorus seems to often be helpful for cats, regardless of the situation.

Some of the most spectacular responses I've seen have been with cats. Make sure you understand how to use remedies. Low potencies or LMs are best when the patient is debilitated. Having had both good and bad experiences being self-taught I'd suggest getting a homeopathic vet's help if you can. If not, maybe we here can help.

comments.title: DID and Thanksgiving to Sekhmet
comments.content:
I think possibly I'm in the process of forming a new personality that's basically there to get around my inhibitions about becoming a very self destructive lifestyle submissive (in the kinky sense)...a type of submission heavily laden with "sacrificing to the dominant" sex magic.

I suppose it's possibly there's some sort of glamour from the dominant and I'm mistaking it for Dissociative Identity Disorder.

But the reason I say DID is because I am long familiar what it's like to wrestle with temptation, say, to hold to my dietary restrictions. (Which are very stringent.) And what it feels like to eat something I shouldn't despite knowing very well the consequences will be dire. (And I feel in my body those consequences faintly and sometimes not so faintly very soon after.)

Versus what goes on with the "sliding toward giving myself to the dominant" stuff. Where it's like, I have intervals where resistance vanishes. Because all the underlying values that are the basis of resisting don't feel present. it's not that I feel and believe them but I'm doing the wrong thing regardless. I'm doing the wrong thing because it genuinely feels right all of the sudden. My whole interpretation of my life experience and my history with the dominant (which goes back 20 years on and off, all long distance thankfully)changes.

(And when I switch back to "normal me" I am instantly appalled at what I've done re: sliding toward. It is difficult to believe I was doing and thinking the things I was just doing and thinking.)

So if I were correct and my core psyche is undergoing some radical restructuring, is there a way to address this directly with ritual magic? Do you have any suggestions for a particular measure that might help immediately? Is there an area you've discussed before I should look at particularly closely?

(I confess haven't been keeping up with your writing for about three years. I come here mostly from having read the blog on magic you did for a while before you started this blog.)

Secondly, I have reason to be very thankful to Sekhmet. I am absolutely convinced She helped me recently and repeatedly in resisting this temptation with the dominant.

My path is not normally one that involves Kemetic spirituality. I don't have anything against it (indeed, there's a lot about it I find interesting and good). But a couple of my friends are close to Her.

So I was surprised that first, each of my friends had a particular way they brought Her closer to me. Which helped even when I wasn't really trying that hard to accept that help.

And second, it turns out that Sekhmet is staunchly opposed to the dominant...because one time he was trying to submit to someone himself, and the relationship suddenly ended because their dominant was a follower of Sekhmet, and Sekhmet vetoed it.

(After the guy had bought a very expensive collar for the dominant I'm trying to resist, in fact. So clearly there is at least a strong possibility this other dominant was not using Sekhmet as a pretext.)

So any suggestions either how I should structure a ritual to thank Her?

(Does anyone into the Kemetic path have some suggstions? Or do you know anyone on that path who might pass some advice on?)

replies.title: Re: DID and Thanksgiving to Sekhmet
replies.content: In my opinion you need to seek professional psychological help as soon as possible. That's the only advice I feel is appropriate in this situation.

comments.title: gods and belief
comments.content:
I seem to recall that you commented some years ago about the idea that the gods rely on human belief and will fade away once forgotten. This turns up in several fantasy authors--Pratchett and Gaiman and others. Can you trace an origin for this idea and comment on it or direct me to other sources. Thanks.

Rita

replies.title: Re: gods and belief
replies.content: I'm not at all sure where that idea comes from. I hope I made it clear in that earlier discussion that I consider it to be complete malarkey...

replies.title: Re: gods and belief
replies.content: There was a Star Trek episode where they meet Apollo and after they tell him they’ve outgrown him he fades away on the wind, so the idea goes back at least to the mid-1960’s.

replies.title: Re: gods and belief
replies.content:
Yes, I recalled that you thought it was nonsense, but I couldn't remember whether you had added, something like, 'this idea appears in the works of little known Victorian occultist, John X, but seems to have become a persistent one in the West."

---wait a minute here--Kipling?? there is a shadow of the idea in "Puck of Pook's Hill"--with the gods coming to England and dwindling into spirits and fairies before disappearing. And Gaiman pays homage to Kipling by having the real Puck stay behind in Elizabethan England when the rest of Oberon's court withdraw in the episode of The Sandman in which Shakespeare's company presents Midsummer Night's Dream to the real Fairy court under Dream's supervision.

But Kipling and later Gaiman are already presenting the idea in fictional form, not as serious occult theory. It is obviously related to the truism that the gods of one religion become the demons or sprites of the next--but it isn't quite the same. An out and out atheist would not believe that human constructs ever had real power in the material world, while a theist would not believe that the gods relied on human minds for their existence.

More research.

Rita
